title: Paid Subscription
description: You signed up for a subscription to gain more card packs and energy. But signing up is the easy part — leaving is the tricky part. The cancellation process forces you through long help lines or confusing webpages.
whyItWorks: Signing up is made frictionless and appealing, while cancellation is intentionally tedious. If the effort to cancel feels worse than the cost of staying, users will continue paying over time.
solution: Always check the cancellation process before subscribing. More importantly, ask yourself if you actually need the subscription at all.

title: Limited Time Deal
description: You were pressured into buying a limited-time deal. It seemed valuable, but how much of that urgency was artificial?
whyItWorks: Time pressure alters decision-making and inflates perceived value, making offers feel “just worth it.”
solution: Slow down. Ask yourself if you really need the boost now, or if you could progress naturally over time.

title: Energy Bundle
description: You bought an energy bundle to play more and progress faster. But was skipping the wait actually worth the cost?
whyItWorks: Small, immediate gains feel worth it in the moment, but repeated purchases add up significantly over time.
solution: Ask yourself if the need is urgent. Waiting is often the better long-term option.

title: Watched an Ad
description: You watched an ad to open a pack. It felt like a small trade, but all that time adds up.
whyItWorks: Ads are normalized in games, making them easy to accept. Short or interactive ads reduce resistance.
solution: Consider whether the reward is worth your time. Time has value too.

title: Sharing Your Profile
description: You shared your collection with others. It feels rewarding, but how much of that value was shaped by the game?
whyItWorks: Social validation turns progress into status, encouraging continued engagement.
solution: It’s fine to be proud, but don’t let perceived status pressure you into playing more than you want.

title: Opening Card Packs
description: You opened packs to get better cards. It feels necessary, but the system is intentionally designed that way.
whyItWorks: It mimics gambling mechanics. The excitement of randomness becomes the main driver.
solution: If you can’t control the urge to open packs, consider stepping away from the game.

title: Recurring Emails Sign-up
description: When creating an account, email notifications are often enabled by default.
whyItWorks: Users rush through sign-ups and don’t notice default selections.
solution: Take a moment to review default settings before finishing registration.

title: Multiple In-Game Currencies
description: Games use multiple currencies, making it hard to determine value.
whyItWorks: Complexity discourages comparison, leading users to overspend.
solution: Do the math. If it’s intentionally confusing, reconsider spending.

title: Connect to Google Account
description: Apps repeatedly prompt users to connect accounts for convenience.
whyItWorks: Convenience reduces resistance, while increasing data tracking.
solution: Evaluate whether convenience is worth the loss of privacy.

title: Low Contrast Text
description: When services ask users for consent to make a purchase, but deliberately hide information using colours that blend into the background screen.
whyItWorks: While technically not breaking any laws, users are still likely to miss out on critical information that will affect their purchasing decision
solution: Before buying or signing up for a service, double check any information given. If necessary, turn the brightness up or manually adjust contrast

title: End-User License Agreements
description: Many services, like Google, know they’re essential components of a user’s modern life, letting them ask for unreasonable demands in their EULA’s.
whyItWorks: Users need services like a Google account to effectively operate in modern society. If the EULA is large enough, most users won’t actually read through them
solution: This is a tricky one, because for necessary services you’re basically forced to accept the terms. Try to find a summary somewhere of what you signed away. If possible, seriously assess if the cost in privacy and data is worth the convenience, or if an alternative service is available.
